metadata.threat_model_notes: Codra handles AI provider API credentials and asset bundles. High-value targets: user stored API keys, proprietary project blueprints, and asset manifests. Vectors: Exposed/unauthenticated serverless functions, bypassing RLS using service keys, and IDORs.

findings.title: Critical Authentication Bypass in asset_manifest_save.ts
findings.description: The netlify function `asset_manifest_save.ts` uses `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` to bypass Row Level Security. It trusts the `userId` and `workspaceId` extracted directly from the request payload JSON without validating any JWT or Authorization header. An attacker can forge save requests for any user or workspace, overwriting their assets.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker sends a POST request to `/api/asset_manifest_save` with `{ "userId": "target-uuid", "workspaceId": "target-workspace-id", "manifest": {...} }`. The server blindly trusts these parameters and overwrites the victim's asset bundle using its admin privileges.
findings.fix: Use `await supabase.auth.getUser(token)` to verify the user's identity from the `Authorization` header instead of trusting the request body payload. Alternatively, rely on RLS and use the user's initialized Supabase client rather than the service role key.

findings.title: Unauthenticated Data Exposure in asset_manifest_get.ts
findings.description: The netlify function `asset_manifest_get.ts` fetches asset bundles and assets using the `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` (RLS bypassed) by simply taking a `bundleId` from the query string. There is no session validation.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker sends a GET request to `/api/asset_manifest_get?bundleId=predictable-or-leaked-id`. The server responds with the full manifest contents, leaking proprietary assets.
findings.fix: Implement JWT validation via `supabase.auth.getUser(token)` and verify the authenticated user has access to the workspace that owns the bundle, or use the regular Supabase client to rely on RLS.
